speaker: interviewer
text: Schneid-Ofseyer

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a family portrait, a family of the four of us. You see only three, but this was an innovation of Otto Schneid, the [? optimism. ?] He said that when you're looking at the world, you see more than you are painting. You see parts of your nose, you see parts of your eyebrows. And this was the way he decided to paint, and he has many paintings in that method, showing that here you have-- on the right side-- you have Yaacov. On the left side you have Adam. In the center, you have me. And the hand of Otto is on the shoulder of Adam, of Moshe-Adam. And the white lines that you see is Otto, because this is the way he saw himself. I wanted to mention that when we were in Firenze in 1960, when Otto had there an exhibition in the [? Galleria d'Arte Internazionale, ?] [INAUDIBLE], the publisher of the old masters, came to him after the Pitti Palace, or actually the [? Fitzi ?] Gallery, got the sculpture that I mentioned before. And they offered him, or suggested, to make for him a portfolio of his art. They had only the old masters so far. Otto Schneid was the first contemporary artist to be in their beautiful edition. And this painting is one of them. It is a double portrait of Otto and myself many years ago. It was done in 1958. And it is kind of embracing without touching, blessing, from both of us. And his side and then my side, with lots of concentration.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a huge painting, five by six. 'Jacob's Dream'. The biblical Jacob is dreaming, but it's model was our son, {?Jacob?]. And he's lying and dreaming with open eyes. Instead of a ladder-- like in Jacob's dream in Genesis-- you have a ladder of human beings coming down and climbing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these human beings have portraits. They are real, like his mother, his sister on the right-hand side. And on top, my father. These figures who have a speck of red in them are people who were martyrs who were killed during the Holocaust. And all of these people are coming to bless Jacob. And all the mood in the picture is like the feeling of a dream. This is also a very large painting, six by five and a half. It is called 'David - [? Orpheus' ?]. His model is our son, Moshe-Adam. And he is playing as if he were David. He was supposed to play on the harp, but he's playing on the cello, and this is because Otto loved cellos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by his music he is attracting the beasts of the end of days that are not going to kill each other anymore, they will live happily. On the top, you can see the darkness. It is night, but underneath is already day. The day's approaching, the day's coming. And I'm a little worried about the wolf down there. He listens to the music, but it's a great danger. If the child will stop playing for a second, then who knows what will happen? This is Job. Remember the story? That God was testing him and he took away everything that Job had. His children were killed, his fortune sunk in the ocean. He, himself, got all kinds of wounds all over his body and he was sitting and scratching himself and three friends came to comfort him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not according to what is in the book. Otto gave himself some permission to change a little. Because in the book Job, which is a fascinating book, these three figures are very similar to one another and they say almost the same things. But here, if you see on the left side, there is that one will raises his hand and says, God knows why he punished you. You probably deserve it.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right hand, you have one who is full of heart and mercy. And in the middle is a little one who doesn't know. Who is he to say who is right, and what is right, and what are the sins of Job?

speaker: interviewee
text: This painting, it's a large painting, was painted in three days, but three days day and night. The idea to this painting was the beginning of his model to the six millions, the phoenix. The phoenix is supposed to live a thousand years, to be burned by fire, and to come out the ashes and live another thousand years. It's an eternal life. So this was a mirror on our wall. And you can see here the fire and the bird coming out the fire to live a new life, they symbol of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The wall is where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wall was in our apartment in Hifar in [INAUDIBLE], and I don't know if it still exists. Four figures. This is a picture depicting the Holocaust. The land is empty. The figures are white. The sky is falling down. And it's not a scream, it's stronger than a scream. Look what happened to human beings. Look what is happening to human beings. This pastel is also in [INAUDIBLE] portfolio. It depicts a Hassid, a Jew of today who is concentrated in himself. And the yellow on his eyebrow is telling you about enlightenment, about light that is inside. But the eyes are not even open, they are closed. And the face has all these red spots that are telling you about martyrdom. And this one has another one that goes with it, also a pastel, that we will see right away. This pastel is called 'The Martyr'. Again, the eyes are closed, but the face is telling you about all the sorrows that we, Jewish people, went through. We don't have to tell you what is the connection to the Holocaust. You feel it. Eleanor Roosevelt wrote in this letter that you can see here that she's not sure if she will be able to come to the first exhibit of Otto Schneid, but she did. And you can see her there. And this was the painting that she got for the FDR Memorial Library in Hyde Park, New York, and it is still there. Dr. Hermann Maas, whom you see here, was one of the [SPEAKING HEBREW], the righteous among the gentiles. He was the [? prolat ?] of the Protestant Church in Heidelberg and he himself saved hundreds of Jewish children and sent them to England. Then he was caught by the Nazis and spent some time in Auschwitz. Luckily, he survived. And when he came to Israel to visit all this many friends that he had, for-- in the eyes of Otto-- was it a must to see Dr. Hermann Maas, and we invited him and he came to us. We found out that this man is lighting candles every Friday night for a lot of the Protestant Church and goes every Rosh Hashana and Yom Kippur to the Jewish synagogue, prays covered with the tallit, and knows Hebrew very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were corresponding afterwards and I have an envelope filled up with letters by Dr. Hermann Maas and I translated part of it into English. From a letter by Dr. Hermann Maas, 1960. 'My very, very beloved friends Miriam and Naftali, why am I so far from you? I feel so lonely when I'm not with Jews. In my heart, I am a Jew. Whatever I could give you would be returned seven folds. But true love doesn't count and doesn't repay. It always gives plenty. And this is the greatest happiness, am I right? It is quiet around me, and my memories go to you. And they are so sweet, like love. To be today, on this Sabbath day, with you again, like a Jew, it is like a delicate song. This would mean my being in my birthplace. Please take me to you and I will hear your poems, beloved Miriam. You are a deep poetess. I will also see your masterpieces, my brother, Naftali. In that way, I will find the greatest fulfillment of my life, of my life, to enter Judaism, my great nation.'

speaker: interviewee
text: We need-- this letter was written by Senator Morse in 1966 after he saw a portfolio of Otto Schneid. And in the center paragraph, he says very touching and very honest things. He says, 'because we are both in about the same age, I can compare my achievements to your achievements. My achievements in the politics are going to disappear in a couple of years, but your achievements are going to stay forever.' This is a letter of condolences from Premier Davis after Otto died. And you can read the beautiful words that he puts there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The premier from--

speaker: interviewee
text: The premier from Ontario. This is my husband, Rabbi Dr. Leon Ofseyer. I call him Lover, because this is a man that the main part of his life is love. For example, a day like today when we were so busy the whole day, he didn't complain, didn't say a thing, just worried about me. One book that I wrote about him is a book of love poems, and I wrote it in Hebrew called [INAUDIBLE],

speaker: interviewer
text: And then this book was translated and Lover translated it for me. And there are many beautiful poems here that I just love. Shall I read one of them? It's called 'Miracles'.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'I sing of the miracle of being alive, of the miracles that you are real, of the miracle that you are with me. I sing of the miracles that our day is as festive as our night, of the miracle of tranquility that fills my skies, of the miracle that my skies seems to be only like a canopy over us, and all the Earth folded and was in our home, and all the melodies were contained in this one melody that we sing together when the day's work ends. And the miracle of this melody I sing my song.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my better half. [GIGGLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have always a dispute. We call it in the [INAUDIBLE], in the Talmud, [INAUDIBLE]. Why? She says I'm the better half, but I know she is the better half

speaker: interviewee
text: So we fight. We fight about it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: However, we are married close to 20 years, and thank the Lord, we never had any confrontation. Why did I say confrontation? Miriam and I were in Israel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were over to our relative. He asked us, after a conversation-- he was a writer and a very intelligent man-- [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have a confrontation? I says, no. We never had a confrontation. We love each other. Why confront each other? For what? And that is close to 20 years. Of course, we are getting younger, but that's natural. The biological clock is working--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] And we are in the same boat as anybody else. We hope to God that the Lord will give us strength, health, and we will continue in the same way. Amen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Yaacov Schneid, Otto Schneid's older son of two children. This is my wife. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name is Tsila. We have three daughters, one of which is here. The other two are vacationing with the other grandparents. I'm sorry that I never met Otto Schneid, but through his work and his painting, his art, I feel like I'm lucky enough to have gotten that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Adina Schneid, the middle daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's both an honor and also hard for me to be here. I'm flooded with memories, both cherished memories and also sad ones. My father's artwork has always been an inspiration to me. His life, I think, exemplified both the pain that he felt personally losing his family, his dearest mother, and sister, and many other relatives. At the same time, he also lived a life full of hope and optimism for the future. And this balance is I think what stands out in my mind very strongly and has influenced me a great deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What we're going to see now is the treasure. Miriam, can you tell us, once again, a bit about the treasure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll try. This is a story of a book that was never published, but this book caused so many things. First of all, it saved Otto's life. If not that treasure, he would be probably staying in Austria, or been killed, or going through the Holocaust and killed with his mother and family. As I mentioned before, he wanted to show Hitler what is Jewish art. And because of that, he went from one [INAUDIBLE] to another, from one studio to another, to see the greatest artists of the '30s. And he got from them photographs. And all of these photographs were with him. 160 over those were with a book that he wrote in German. And the preface was written by Martin [? Blumberg. ?] When the Nazis occupied Austria, and confiscated the book and put him on the black list. Because of that, because he was on the black list, he had to hide in Warsaw. And he was hiding for six months, but then you fell into the hands of the Pols, of the police, and it was three days in prison. And afterwards, during the days when he was in prison, he got the certificate from the Hebrew University to be a research student, although he had already his doctorate. So he had to leave Poland. He was forced to leave Poland. He wouldn't like to do that, but he was forced to because it was either leaving Poland or dying. And I mentioned that he had just one day see his family and to say goodbye, and he took with him a suitcase, and in that suitcase were all the photographs that he had. The others, not the ones that we're supposed to be in the book. And also a copy of the manuscript. Then when he came to Israel and translated the book into Hebrew, called it [INAUDIBLE], and this book did not appear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it is a whole tragedy of a book that was written, full of information and of knowledge, and it never saw any publisher. After Otto's death, I went through the papers, his papers, and it was just unbelievable. I found about 5,000 pieces of letters, photographs, catalogs, old books of artists and whatnot. But-- and then I went through all of this with my husband, with Leon. And we wanted to do something with it. What is it going to do in our closet? So we found somebody who wanted to buy this collection and to donate it to an institution in Toronto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The institution was very, very interested in getting it. And that man sent it to the-- I believe that it was called C33 Committee and they declared that this is cultural property, that this is a national treasure. And the man could have bought it, but he was afraid. His lawyer told him that if he will donate something that is worth $150,000-- it's about 15 years ago-- then the income tax might look into his affairs and he did not like the idea. And that's why it fell through. Never mind. But the main thing about this treasure is that when you look at this list, you see so many artists whose name were forgotten, whose art disappeared. And I don't know who else has letters and photographs of the art of these artists, but we do have.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many artists are in the treasure?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the treasure are 167 artists, and there are 374 letters from the artists. And the photos, the artwork, 1,098. And besides this, to get to the 5,000, you have still a long way, all kinds of other material that is not related directly to the Holocaust, but efforts that Otto put in to save the world, to save Israel, to do something. He used to have copies of the letters that he wrote and he was a wonderful letter writer.

speaker: interviewer
text: We should make a point to emphasize that some of the art that we are going to see now, we don't know very much about the artists. Were just going to be looking at some photographs, some of the correspondence between Otto and them. And these are just a small sample of the treasure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And if there are, for example, 10 photos, we took out one. So that this is just an idea. This is waiting for a place that is going to house it and to put it in such a stage that everybody will be able to go through it and to see. The first letter that you see with three photos is by [? Nathan ?] [? Schpiegel. ?] It is written in 1936 to Otto Schneid and it's written in Yiddish. The three pictures that you can see here, on the right is a [INAUDIBLE]. On the left is a [INAUDIBLE], and in the center is a picture of [? Nathan ?] [? Schpiegel. ?] Unfortunately, I don't know who [? Nathan ?] Schpiegel was. I hope that some other people would know and would have, perhaps, some more material, but we have many more pictures and another letter from [? Nathan ?] [? Schpiegel ?] in our collection. This letter came from France, from [? Leon ?] [? Druker ?] in 1931. He was a sculptor and these two pieces of sculpture were exhibited in one of the best salons in Paris. The letter is written in French, right. Anna Neagoe. She was born in Russia and then afterwards went to the United States. But what I see in the envelope from where we took out some of the things that you can see right here, we have one invitation to an exhibition from 1929, another invitation from 1931, seven write-ups, one article by Otto Schneid, one letter from 1931, and a description of one of her other paintings, besides other photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm just bringing a little of them to give you an idea because it is a monumental collection that we have. In addition, I believe in the article that we see here from it mentions that Anna was living in Paris at the time in the '30's.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, OK. This is an autobiography by Anna Neagoe. And she wrote in detail about her life, where she was born, what she was doing, where she studied and so on, when she married and whom. And it was written from Paris in 1931. This is page two of the autobiography. And we have of many artists-- and most of them I don't know-- autobiographies and photographs of the way they looked when they were still alive. And all the rest that is really, that really needs a research. Page three of the autobiography of Anna Neagoe. This is [? Jacob ?] [? Plesner ?]. From Berlin in 1930, he wrote that letter and sent the photographs. He was a sculptor and I see on the envelope that there is one letter here without a date, one letter from the 27th of November, 1930, and another letter, the fourth of December without a date, and 17 photos in Portfolio Number 21. And we are showing you here just three of them. We have three paintings by [? Bension ?] [? Rabinovich. ?] He sent also his autobiography. He was born in 1905 in Białystok, Poland, and he exhibited, started to exhibit in 1921. He had exhibits in many galleries. He got a stipend, yeah, a scholarship, from in the Białystok, in Białystok. And there are some more details about his biography that you will see in a minute. That's a letter from [? Bension ?] [? Rabinovich ?] from Paris. In 1930 he wrote it in Yiddish. And on the left side is his autobiography. This is a letter. The two pages are one letter from [? Rosenyetski ?] from Paris, 1930, in Yiddish. These four paintings are also by [? Rosenyetski. ?] He's a serious painter. On the right hand you see the [INAUDIBLE], the man who believes in God. Next to it, below, you see hunger. Then you have a [? eternal ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And on top are [INAUDIBLE], or souls who are suffering. I don't remember now how because it's in Yiddish, suffering souls. This painting is by [? Rimer. ?] This letter is in German, 1930. In our collection, we have letters from '32, '30, '30' '30, '32 and '31. On the 17th of November, Otto wrote an article about him and we have 12 photos in the album Number 21. So we show you just one of them.
